summary: Shapiro recounts his experience with a cheap Ryobi bandsaw that proved useless for cutting curved pieces from thick lumber but turned out to be perfect for trimming splines on picture frames. The blade wandered too much for precision work on 8/4 stock but handled the delicate spline-trimming task cleanly. He shares a practical tip from a friend about starting flush-cut saw cuts from the loose end to avoid breaking spline corners. The post is a lighthearted reminder that the right tool for the job isn't always the best tool — sometimes a junky tool finds its niche.
key_points: A cheap bandsaw that fails at heavy cutting tasks can excel at lighter, more forgiving work like spline trimming
When trimming splines with a flush-cut saw, always start cutting from the loose hanging end to avoid breaking the corner
The post illustrates the maker principle of finding unexpected uses for imperfect tools rather than discarding them
money_quotes: The sawblade was wandering around the lumber like a drunken fratboy on mardi gras.
I ran the only-slightly-bloodied edge of that old picture frame along the junky bandsaw blade, and it was a match made in heaven.
The darn thing sheared off perfectly.
Be very careful to start cutting at the loose hanging end. If you don't, then you risk breaking off the tip of the corner of the spline as you finish the second cut.
I mentioned it in my post of a few days ago, not actually realizing that I did have a functional-albeit-crappy bandsaw in my basement at the time. Oh well, I learned.
key_insight: An imperfect tool isn't a useless tool — matching the right task to the right (even crappy) equipment can yield surprisingly good results.
